title: Perianal Abscess & Fistula
oneLiner: Acute infection of anal glands forming a pus cavity in the perianal space; frequent in young males; requires prompt surgical drainage to prevent sepsis and fistula formation.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 28-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe, throbbing perianal pain that has worsened over 48 hours. He cannot sit comfortably and is shifting weight. He has a low-grade fever (38.2°C) and chills. On examination, there is a tender, fluctuant swelling at the 6 o'clock position near the anal verge, with overlying erythema and warmth. He is otherwise healthy, no significant past medical history.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **Severe, throbbing perianal pain** – suggests an abscess under tension; pain is often out of proportion to visible signs.
- **Cannot sit** – mechanical disruption of the anorectal sling; hallmark of anorectal infection.
- **Low-grade fever and chills** – systemic inflammatory response to deep-seated infection; abscess is a source of bacteraemia.
- **Fluctuant swelling** – pus collection in the intersphincteric or ischiorectal space; fluctuance confirms liquid content under pressure.
- **Erythema and warmth** – local inflammation; infection spreading to overlying skin.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **The "silent" abscess in diabetes** – a poorly controlled diabetic may present with only mild discomfort but have a large, deep abscess (ischiorectal or supralevator). No external fluctuance; pain is vague and referred to the sacrum or hip. Key cue: unexplained fever, leukocytosis, and perianal tenderness on deep palpation.
- **Crohn's disease – multiple, "painless" abscesses** – patient may have multiple small perianal abscesses that are surprisingly non-tender due to chronic inflammation and fistulation. Look for perianal skin tags, buttock crease erythema, and a history of abdominal pain or diarrhoea. The abscess may be a superficial manifestation of a deep fistulous track.
- **Postpartum abscess** – a young woman 2–4 weeks postpartum presents with perianal pain and swelling that is mistaken for episiotomy breakdown or hemorrhoid. Always consider an intersphincteric abscess after vaginal delivery; it can track along superficial transverse perineal muscles.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
A perianal abscess is a collection of pus that forms within the soft tissues surrounding the anal canal, most commonly arising from infection of the anal glands (cryptoglandular theory). The infection starts in the anal crypt, tracks through the internal sphincter into the intersphincteric plane, and then spreads along predictable anatomical pathways to form abscesses in various perianal spaces (intersphincteric, ischiorectal, supralevator, or horseshoe).

A fistula-in-ano is the chronic phase: an abnormal epithelialised tract that connects the anal canal (typically at the dentate line) to the perianal skin. Up to 50% of perianal abscesses will develop a fistula if not adequately drained, or if the abscess recurs.

**Why this matters:** Perianal abscess is one of the most common colorectal surgical emergencies. It is easily misdiagnosed as "hemorrhoids" or "piles" by junior clinicians, leading to delayed drainage, sepsis, or progression to necrotising fasciitis (Fournier's gangrene). The stakes are high: a missed or inadequately drained abscess can cause life-threatening perineal sepsis, and an unrecognised fistula can lead to recurrent abscess, complex fistulous tracks, and faecal incontinence after surgery.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of development:**

1. **Cryptoglandular origin:** The anal canal has 6–14 anal glands that empty into crypts at the dentate line. These glands produce mucus to lubricate stool. When the crypt becomes obstructed (by faecal matter, trauma, or infection), the gland becomes infected with gut flora (E. coli, Bacteroides, Streptococcus). Pus forms behind the obstruction.

2. **Extension along planes:** The infection cannot drain because the duct is blocked. It tracks through the internal anal sphincter (the weakest point) into the **intersphincteric plane** (between the internal and external sphincters). This is the "open door" to all other spaces. From there it can go:
   - Downward and outward – forming a **perianal abscess** (most common, superficial).
   - Laterally through the external sphincter – **ischiorectal abscess** (often large, horseshoe).
   - Upward between the levator ani and the rectal wall – **supralevator abscess** (rare, presents with deep pelvic pain and urinary symptoms).

3. **Abscess formation:** Pus accumulation under pressure causes the severe pain and fluctuant swelling. The body mounts an inflammatory response: neutrophils infiltrate, causing erythema, warmth, and systemic fever.

4. **Natural history (untreated):** The abscess will either:
   - Spontaneously drain through the skin into the anal canal (forming a fistula), or
   - Spread along fascial planes (especially dangerous in the ischiorectal space, where it can become a horseshoe abscess extending circumferentially), or
   - In immunocompromised patients, cause necrotising fasciitis (Fournier’s gangrene) – a surgical emergency.

**Why each sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|-----------|---------------|
| Pus accumulation in closed space | **Severe, throbbing pain** – pain receptors in the sphincter and skin are stretched. Pain is constant, worse with sitting/Valsalva (increased pressure). |
| Inflammation of overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue | **Erythema, warmth, swelling** – vasodilation and oedema. |
| Systemic immune response (IL-6, TNF-alpha) | **Fever, chills, raised WCC/CRP** – the body recognises the abscess as a source of bacteraemia. |
| Involvement of the pudendal nerve (if abscess extends into ischiorectal fossa) | **Referred pain to the testicle/labia** – due to shared nerve supply (S2-S4). |
| Internal sphincter spasm (reflex) | **Anal tenderness and difficulty on rectal exam** – the patient cannot tolerate any pressure. |
| Infection tracking through the external sphincter | **Fluctuance in the ischiorectal fossa** – pus is deeper, often not visible externally, but a bulge can be felt on bimanual exam. |

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**

- The "young male" pattern – anal gland anatomy is constant; young men have higher rates of cryptoglandular infection, possibly due to increased anal sphincter tone and harder stools.
- "Pain out of proportion to external findings" – deep abscess in ischiorectal or supralevator space: there is little external swelling but massive deep pus. The patient will have severe perianal pain with minimal visible erythema. Always palpate externally and perform a gentle rectal exam.
- "Diabetes or immunosuppression" – patients are at higher risk of necrotising fasciitis because the infection spreads more rapidly along fascial planes due to impaired neutrophil function. Also, they may have atypical organisms (Pseudomonas, anaerobes) that cause subtle signs until late.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common, ~70%) | Cryptoglandular infection – no clear precipitant | Young healthy male, single abscess, no systemic illness |
| **Infectious** | Secondary to local infection – perianal cellulitis from skin breaks, fissure, or trauma | Recent diarrhoea, fissure, or childbirth |
| **Inflammatory bowel disease** | Crohn’s disease (less commonly UC) – transmural inflammation leads to deep fistula and abscess | Multiple abscesses, perianal skin tags, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, weight loss |
| **Immunocompromised** | Diabetes mellitus, HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy, steroids | Poor glycaemic control (HbA1c >8), or known HIV, or recent chemo |
| **Post-surgical / trauma** | After hemorrhoidectomy, episiotomy, or foreign body | Recent anorectal surgery or obstetric tear |
| **Rare / systemic** | Tuberculosis, actinomycosis, hidradenitis suppurativa | Chronic draining sinuses, "water-can" perineum, inguinal lymphadenopathy |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic cryptoglandular infection. This is the default assumption in an otherwise healthy patient.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring:** There is no validated scoring system for perianal abscess. Diagnosis is clinical. However, the Parks classification is used for fistula (useful for surgical planning). For acute abscess, **clinical examination is the gold standard** – if you see a fluctuant mass with overlying erythema and the patient has pain, you have made the diagnosis. Do not wait for imaging.

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **None (if classic)** | – | History and exam are sufficient for superficial perianal abscess. Do not delay drainage. | – |
| **Bloods (FBC, CRP, U&E, lactate)** | WCC elevated, CRP high, lactate may be normal or elevated | To assess severity of sepsis and identify organ dysfunction (NEWS2). Also check glucose (unmask diabetes). | WCC >12 × 10⁹/L, CRP >100 mg/L indicate significant infection. Lactate >2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion – escalate. |
| **MRI Pelvis** | High-resolution T2-weighted images show abscess cavity, fistulous track, extension into ischiorectal or supralevator space | When the abscess is deep, recurrent, or suspected complex fistula. Also useful in Crohn’s disease to assess disease activity. | A "horseshoe" abscess – pus crossing the midline posteriorly – indicates an ischiorectal abscess that needs urgent drainage under anaesthesia. For fistula: Parks classification (intersphincteric, transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, extrasphincteric). |
| **Examination under anaesthesia (EUA)** | Direct visualisation and probing of fistula | If a fistula is suspected during I&D, EUA allows identification and primary fistulotomy (if safe). | Finding the internal opening at the dentate line confirms fistula-in-ano. **The Goodsall rule** helps predict direction: anterior fistulas tend to be straight, posterior fistulas tend to curve. |
| **Ultrasound (endoanal)** | Shows intersphincteric abscess, internal opening | Limited role – operator-dependent. Can be used in clinic as a triage tool. | An anechoic or hypoechoic collection in the intersphincteric space is diagnostic. |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out:**

- **Confirms abscess:** Fluctuance on palpation (even if not seen externally). If there is no fluctuance but severe pain, suspect deep abscess and proceed to EUA or imaging.
- **Rules out abscess:** A soft, non-tender perianal area with normal skin color and no systemic signs – consider hemorrhoids or fissure.

**Important:** Do not order MRI or US for a simple superficial abscess – it wastes time and delays definitive drainage.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – the "golden hour"**

| Action | Reasoning |
|--------|-----------|
| **Assess severity** (NEWS2, lactate, signs of necrotising fasciitis) | Identify those who need emergency theatre (sepsis, immunocompromise, crepitus). |
| **IV access and fluids** (500 mL Hartmann's bolus if hypotensive or lactate >2) | Resuscitate to preserve perfusion. |
| **Analgesia** (IV paracetamol 1 g + morphine 5 mg slow IV, titrate) | Pain control for examination. |
| **Incision and drainage (I&D)** – perform under local or general anaesthesia in the ED if trained, or refer to surgical team | This is the **only definitive treatment**. Antibiotics alone are insufficient. The abscess must be drained to relieve pressure, remove pus, and allow healing. |
| **Send pus for culture and sensitivity** (only if immunocompromised or severe sepsis) | Identifies resistant organisms (MRSA, anaerobes) – guides targeted antibiotics if needed. |

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not prescribe antibiotics without drainage – they may suppress systemic signs but the abscess will still progress and can lead to sepsis.
- Do not pack the wound tightly after I&D – a small gauze wick left in for 24 hours is acceptable to keep the wound open, but tight packing causes pain and delays healing. Many surgeons now leave wounds open to drain freely.
- Do not probe the wound aggressively for a fistula in the acute setting – it can create a false track. Fistula assessment is best done 6–8 weeks later.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – after successful I&D**

| Action | Reasoning |
|--------|-----------|
| **Wound care** – daily sitz baths (warm water, no soap) 2–3 times daily | Helps cleanse the wound, reduce swelling, and promote drainage. |
| **Pain control** – regular paracetamol 1 g QDS PO, plus ibuprofen 400 mg TDS PO (if no renal impairment) | NSAIDs help with inflammation. |
| **Antibiotics** – only if signs of cellulitis (erythema extending >2 cm from wound) or systemic sepsis. **Co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS PO for 5 days** (if penicillin allergy, use metronidazole 400 mg TDS + ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD PO). | Treat associated cellulitis but do not use for superficial abscess alone after adequate drainage. |
| **Monitor for recurrence** – if the wound closes rapidly and pain returns, consider re-accumulation or missed loculation. | Need re-examination and possible re-drainage. |
| **Arrange follow-up** in 2 weeks for wound check. If signs of fistula (persistent purulent discharge, repeated abscess), refer to colorectal surgeon. | Early fistula detection improves outcomes. |

**Long-term – fistula management (if develops)**

- For simple intersphincteric or low transsphincteric fistula: **fistulotomy** (open the track) – gold standard, high cure rate (>95%) but carries risk of incontinence for minor flatus if the external sphincter is divided >30–50%.
- For complex fistula (high transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, or involving >30% of external sphincter): **seton placement** (a drain holds the track open, allowing healing while preserving sphincter function) – may need staged procedures.
- For Crohn’s-related fistula: medical management first (anti-TNF agents like infliximab) often reduces abscess formation; surgical seton is used for drainage, not cure.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Prescribing antibiotics for a simple perianal abscess – delays the inevitable and wastes resources.
- Prematurely probing for a fistula during acute I&D – can create a false passage.
- Assuming a "healed" abscess after drainage: if the wound does not close in 4–6 weeks, suspect a fistula.
- Forgetting to check for Fournier’s gangrene in diabetic patients – if they have crepitus, systemic toxicity, or perineal tenderness extending to the scrotum or labia, they need urgent debridement and ICU.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Perianal Abscess | Thrombosed External Hemorrhoid | Pilonidal Abscess | Hidradenitis Suppurativa |
|---------|------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Severe, throbbing, constant, worse with sitting | Acute, sharp pain (first 24–48 hours); then gradual improvement | Painful, but often less severe; located over the sacrococcygeal area | Chronic, recurrent painful nodules; mild to moderate pain |
| **Exam** | Tender, fluctuant mass near anal verge; erythema, warmth | Tender, firm, dark blue/red "grape" at anal margin; no fluctuance | Midline, dimple/pit in natal cleft; tender, abscess at base | Multiple, draining sinus openings in perineum, groin, axillae; induration |
| **Fever / SIRS** | Often present (low-grade) | Absent | Occasionally present if abscess is large | Rare – unless secondary infection |
| **Location** | Any perianal space (intersphincteric, ischiorectal, etc.) | Anal margin at dentate line | Sacrococcygeal midline | Perianal, but also inguinal, axillary, breast |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Fluctuance and deep pain; fever | No fluctuance; visible thrombosed vein; self-limiting after 3–5 days | Pilonidal pits in the natal cleft; often chronic | Multiple draining sinus tracts; history of acne or chronic skin abscesses |
| **Imaging** | MRI (if deep) shows abscess cavity | None needed | US/MRI shows pilonidal sinus | US shows tract from hair follicle |

**Mnemonic for perianal mimics: "H.A.P.E."** — Hemorrhoid, Abscess (pilonidal), Perianal abscess (our topic), Extramammary (hidradenitis). But remember the key differentiator: **fluctuance + fever = abscess.**
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Fournier’s gangrene (necrotising fasciitis of the perineum)** | Within 12–24 hours of untreated deep abscess; high fever, hypotension, confusion. | Crepitus on palpation (gas in tissues); skin necrosis (black/grey patches); extreme pain that may later become anaesthetic as nerves die. | **Emergency surgical debridement** – wide excision of all necrotic fascia and skin. IV antibiotics (broad-spectrum – piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin). ICU support. High mortality (20–50%). |
| **Recurrent abscess** | Same site, weeks to months after initial I&D. | Pain returns, new fluctuant mass. | Re-evaluate with MRI for fistula. If fistula present, plan definitive treatment (fistulotomy or seton). |
| **Chronic fistula-in-ano** | Persistent purulent discharge from an opening near the anus; recurrent abscess at same site. | External opening visible; internal opening felt at dentine line on EUA. | Elective fistulotomy or seton. |
| **Faecal incontinence (after surgery)** | Accidental leakage of gas or stool – risk increases with sphincter division >30% of external sphincter. | Patient reports soiling, urgency. After fistulotomy, risk is 5–10% for low fistulas. | Offer sacral nerve stimulation or sphincteroplasty. |
| **Sepsis and multi-organ failure** | High NEWS2, lactate >4, oliguria, confusion. | Tachycardia, hypotension, raised lactate. | Resuscitate with IV fluids, IV antibiotics, source control (drainage). Early ICU. |
| **Supralevator abscess – missed** | Pelvic pain, dysuria, tenesmus, back pain. | External exam normal; rectal exam reveals a tender, boggy mass above the levator. | MRI pelvis; transrectal drainage (under anaesthesia). |

**Late-presentation patterns that test "what happens if untreated":**

- The patient with a perianal abscess who delays seeking care for 5–7 days, then presents with septic shock, crepitus in the perineum, and skin blistering – this is Fournier’s gangrene. Mortality is high even with aggressive debridement. The stem will ask: "What is the most important next step?" – Immediate surgical exploration and debridement, not just antibiotics.
- A patient with a drained abscess that heals but then develops a recurrent abscess at the same site 3 months later – this is a classic progression to fistula. The stem asks: "What investigation is most appropriate?" – MRI pelvis to assess fistula track.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for management of perianal abscess: "DRAIN IT"**

- **D** – Drain (incision and drainage – the only definitive treatment)
- **R** – Resuscitate (IV fluids if septic)
- **A** – Antibiotics (only if cellulitis or immunocompromised)
- **I** – Investigate (if deep or recurrent – MRI)
- **N** – Not pack tightly (leave wound open)
- **I** – Identify fistula (assess later, not at time of drainage)
- **T** – Treat fistula (seton or fistulotomy)

**Mnemonic for causes of perianal abscess: "IDIOT"**

- **I** – Idiopathic (cryptoglandular)
- **D** – Diabetes (immunocompromised)
- **I** – Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's)
- **O** – Obstruction (faecal impaction, anal stenosis)
- **T** – Trauma (surgery, childbirth, foreign body)

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **"Pain out of proportion to external findings" = deep abscess.** Always examine with a gloved finger; if the patient screams with gentle palpation, suspect ischiorectal or supralevator abscess. Get MRI or EUA.
2. **The "horseshoe" abscess clue:** If a patient has bilateral perianal swelling and severe pain, think of a horseshoe abscess (pus tracking circumferentially in the posterior deep space). This requires drainage on both sides, often under general anaesthesia.
3. **Goodsall's rule (for fistula):** An external opening anterior to a line drawn through the ischial tuberosities will have a straight radial track to the anal canal. An opening posterior to this line will have a curved track to the midline posterior crypt. Exception: external openings >3 cm from the anus often have a complex track regardless of position.
4. **Always check for Crohn's disease in young adults with recurrent or multiple abscesses.** Ask about abdominal pain, diarrhoea, weight loss, joint pain, oral ulcers. If you suspect, refer for colonoscopy.
5. **Never prescribe antibiotics alone for a perianal abscess – even if it is "small".** The pus is under pressure and needs to be drained; antibiotics may mask sepsis but will not clear the abscess.
6. **After I&D, tell the patient to return if pain recurs, or if the wound does not close within 4–6 weeks.** That is the signal for a fistula.
7. **Fournier's gangrene is a surgical emergency that does not require imaging to diagnose.** Clinical suspicion (crepitus, bullae, systemic toxicity) is enough to go to theatre.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Calling an abscess a "hemorrhoid"** – hemorrhoids are vascular, not fluctuant; they rarely cause fever. If a patient says "my piles are killing me," always examine.
- **Packing the wound too tightly** – causes more pain and does not improve drainage. A single loop of ribbon gauze is enough – some surgeons do not pack at all.
- **Giving oral antibiotics for a perianal abscess without drainage** – this is a common management error that leads to prolonged infection and sepsis.
- **Failing to recognise Fournier’s gangrene in a diabetic –** if you see a diabetic patient with perianal pain, always check for crepitus and discuss with a senior immediately.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar / consultant) immediately:**

- **NEWS2 score ≥5** or any single red parameter (RR >25, HR >130, SBP <90, SpO2 <92% on air, confusion) – suggests severe sepsis.
- **Lactate ≥4 mmol

## Perianal Abscess & Fistula

**One-liner:** Acute infection of anal glands forming a pus cavity in the perianal space; frequent in young males; requires prompt surgical drainage to prevent sepsis and fistula formation.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 28-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe, throbbing perianal pain that has worsened over 48 hours. He cannot sit comfortably and is shifting weight. He has a low-grade fever (38.2°C) and chills. On examination, there is a tender, fluctuant swelling at the 6 o'clock position near the anal verge, with overlying erythema and warmth. He is otherwise healthy, no significant past medical history.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **Severe, throbbing perianal pain** – suggests an abscess under tension; pain is often out of proportion to visible signs.
- **Cannot sit** – mechanical disruption of the anorectal sling; hallmark of anorectal infection.
- **Low-grade fever and chills** – systemic inflammatory response to deep-seated infection; abscess is a source of bacteraemia.
- **Fluctuant swelling** – pus collection in the intersphincteric or ischiorectal space; fluctuance confirms liquid content under pressure.
- **Erythema and warmth** – local inflammation; infection spreading to overlying skin.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **The "silent" abscess in diabetes** – a poorly controlled diabetic may present with only mild discomfort but have a large, deep abscess (ischiorectal or supralevator). No external fluctuance; pain is vague and referred to the sacrum or hip. Key cue: unexplained fever, leukocytosis, and perianal tenderness on deep palpation.
- **Crohn's disease – multiple, "painless" abscesses** – patient may have multiple small perianal abscesses that are surprisingly non-tender due to chronic inflammation and fistulation. Look for perianal skin tags, buttock crease erythema, and a history of abdominal pain or diarrhoea. The abscess may be a superficial manifestation of a deep fistulous track.
- **Postpartum abscess** – a young woman 2–4 weeks postpartum presents with perianal pain and swelling that is mistaken for episiotomy breakdown or hemorrhoid. Always consider an intersphincteric abscess after vaginal delivery; it can track along superficial transverse perineal muscles.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

A perianal abscess is a collection of pus that forms within the soft tissues surrounding the anal canal, most commonly arising from infection of the anal glands (cryptoglandular theory). The infection starts in the anal crypt, tracks through the internal sphincter into the intersphincteric plane, and then spreads along predictable anatomical pathways to form abscesses in various perianal spaces (intersphincteric, ischiorectal, supralevator, or horseshoe).

A fistula-in-ano is the chronic phase: an abnormal epithelialised tract that connects the anal canal (typically at the dentate line) to the perianal skin. Up to 50% of perianal abscesses will develop a fistula if not adequately drained, or if the abscess recurs.

**Why this matters:** Perianal abscess is one of the most common colorectal surgical emergencies. It is easily misdiagnosed as "hemorrhoids" or "piles" by junior clinicians, leading to delayed drainage, sepsis, or progression to necrotising fasciitis (Fournier's gangrene). The stakes are high: a missed or inadequately drained abscess can cause life-threatening perineal sepsis, and an unrecognised fistula can lead to recurrent abscess, complex fistulous tracks, and faecal incontinence after surgery.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of development:**

1. **Cryptoglandular origin:** The anal canal has 6–14 anal glands that empty into crypts at the dentate line. These glands produce mucus to lubricate stool. When the crypt becomes obstructed (by faecal matter, trauma, or infection), the gland becomes infected with gut flora (E. coli, Bacteroides, Streptococcus). Pus forms behind the obstruction.

2. **Extension along planes:** The infection cannot drain because the duct is blocked. It tracks through the internal anal sphincter (the weakest point) into the **intersphincteric plane** (between the internal and external sphincters). This is the "open door" to all other spaces. From there it can go:
   - Downward and outward – forming a **perianal abscess** (most common, superficial).
   - Laterally through the external sphincter – **ischiorectal abscess** (often large, horseshoe).
   - Upward between the levator ani and the rectal wall – **supralevator abscess** (rare, presents with deep pelvic pain and urinary symptoms).

3. **Abscess formation:** Pus accumulation under pressure causes the severe pain and fluctuant swelling. The body mounts an inflammatory response: neutrophils infiltrate, causing erythema, warmth, and systemic fever.

   - Spontaneously drain through the skin into the anal canal (forming a fistula), or

   - In immunocompromised patients, cause necrotising fasciitis (Fournier’s gangrene) – a surgical emergency.

**Why each sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|-----------|---------------|
| Pus accumulation in closed space | **Severe, throbbing pain** – pain receptors in the sphincter and skin are stretched. Pain is constant, worse with sitting/Valsalva (increased pressure). |
| Inflammation of overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue | **Erythema, warmth, swelling** – vasodilation and oedema. |
| Systemic immune response (IL-6, TNF-alpha) | **Fever, chills, raised WCC/CRP** – the body recognises the abscess as a source of bacteraemia. |
| Involvement of the pudendal nerve (if abscess extends into ischiorectal fossa) | **Referred pain to the testicle/labia** – due to shared nerve supply (S2-S4). |
| Internal sphincter spasm (reflex) | **Anal tenderness and difficulty on rectal exam** – the patient cannot tolerate any pressure. |
| Infection tracking through the external sphincter | **Fluctuance in the ischiorectal fossa** – pus is deeper, often not visible externally, but a bulge can be felt on bimanual exam. |

- "Diabetes or immunosuppression" – patients are at higher risk of necrotising fasciitis because the infection spreads more rapidly along fascial planes due to impaired neutrophil function. Also, they may have atypical organisms (Pseudomonas, anaerobes) that cause subtle signs until late.

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |

| **Immunocompromised** | Diabetes mellitus, HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy, steroids | Poor glycaemic control (HbA1c >8), or known HIV, or recent chemo |

| **Bloods (FBC, CRP, U&E, lactate)** | WCC elevated, CRP high, lactate may be normal or elevated | To assess severity of sepsis and identify organ dysfunction (NEWS2). Also check glucose (unmask diabetes). | WCC >12 × 10⁹/L, CRP >100 mg/L indicate significant infection. Lactate >2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion – escalate. |
| **MRI Pelvis** | High-resolution T2-weighted images show abscess cavity, fistulous track, extension into ischiorectal or supralevator space | When the abscess is deep, recurrent, or suspected complex fistula. Also useful in Crohn’s disease to assess disease activity. | A "horseshoe" abscess – pus crossing the midline posteriorly – indicates an ischiorectal abscess that needs urgent drainage under anaesthesia. For fistula: Parks classification (intersphincteric, transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, extrasphincteric). |
| **Examination under anaesthesia (EUA)** | Direct visualisation and probing of fistula | If a fistula is suspected during I&D, EUA allows identification and primary fistulotomy (if safe). | Finding the internal opening at the dentate line confirms fistula-in-ano. **The Goodsall rule** helps predict direction: anterior fistulas tend to be straight, posterior fistulas tend to curve. |
| **Ultrasound (endoanal)** | Shows intersphincteric abscess, internal opening | Limited role – operator-dependent. Can be used in clinic as a triage tool. | An anechoic or hypoechoic collection in the intersphincteric space is diagnostic. |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out:**

- **Confirms abscess:** Fluctuance on palpation (even if not seen externally). If there is no fluctuance but severe pain, suspect deep abscess and proceed to EUA or imaging.
- **Rules out abscess:** A soft, non-tender perianal area with normal skin color and no systemic signs – consider hemorrhoids or fissure.

**Important:** Do not order MRI or US for a simple superficial abscess – it wastes time and delays definitive drainage.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – the "golden hour"**

| Action | Reasoning |
|--------|-----------|
| **Assess severity** (NEWS2, lactate, signs of necrotising fasciitis) | Identify those who need emergency theatre (sepsis, immunocompromise, crepitus). |
| **IV access and fluids** (500 mL Hartmann's bolus if hypotensive or lactate >2) | Resuscitate to preserve perfusion. |
| **Analgesia** (IV paracetamol 1 g + morphine 5 mg slow IV, titrate) | Pain control for examination. |
| **Incision and drainage (I&D)** – perform under local or general anaesthesia in the ED if trained, or refer to surgical team | This is the **only definitive treatment**. Antibiotics alone are insufficient. The abscess must be drained to relieve pressure, remove pus, and allow healing. |
| **Send pus for culture and sensitivity** (only if immunocompromised or severe sepsis) | Identifies resistant organisms (MRSA, anaerobes) – guides targeted antibiotics if needed. |

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not prescribe antibiotics without drainage – they may suppress systemic signs but the abscess will still progress and can lead to sepsis.
- Do not pack the wound tightly after I&D – a small gauze wick left in for 24 hours is acceptable to keep the wound open, but tight packing causes pain and delays healing. Many surgeons now leave wounds open to drain freely.
- Do not probe the wound aggressively for a fistula in the acute setting – it can create a false track. Fistula assessment is best done 6–8 weeks later.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – after successful I&D**

| Action | Reasoning |
|--------|-----------|
| **Wound care** – daily sitz baths (warm water, no soap) 2–3 times daily | Helps cleanse the wound, reduce swelling, and promote drainage. |
| **Pain control** – regular paracetamol 1 g QDS PO, plus ibuprofen 400 mg TDS PO (if no renal impairment) | NSAIDs help with inflammation. |
| **Antibiotics** – only if signs of cellulitis (erythema extending >2 cm from wound) or systemic sepsis. **Co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS PO for 5 days** (if penicillin allergy, use metronidazole 400 mg TDS + ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD PO). | Treat associated cellulitis but do not use for superficial abscess alone after adequate drainage. |
| **Monitor for recurrence** – if the wound closes rapidly and pain returns, consider re-accumulation or missed loculation. | Need re-examination and possible re-drainage. |
| **Arrange follow-up** in 2 weeks for wound check. If signs of fistula (persistent purulent discharge, repeated abscess), refer to colorectal surgeon. | Early fistula detection improves outcomes. |

**Long-term – fistula management (if develops)**

- For simple intersphincteric or low transsphincteric fistula: **fistulotomy** (open the track) – gold standard, high cure rate (>95%) but carries risk of incontinence for minor flatus if the external sphincter is divided >30–50%.
- For complex fistula (high transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, or involving >30% of external sphincter): **seton placement** (a drain holds the track open, allowing healing while preserving sphincter function) – may need staged procedures.
- For Crohn’s-related fistula: medical management first (anti-TNF agents like infliximab) often reduces abscess formation; surgical seton is used for drainage, not cure.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Prescribing antibiotics for a simple perianal abscess – delays the inevitable and wastes resources.
- Prematurely probing for a fistula during acute I&D – can create a false passage.
- Assuming a "healed" abscess after drainage: if the wound does not close in 4–6 weeks, suspect a fistula.
- Forgetting to check for Fournier’s gangrene in diabetic patients – if they have crepitus, systemic toxicity, or perineal tenderness extending to the scrotum or labia, they need urgent debridement and ICU.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Perianal Abscess | Thrombosed External Hemorrhoid | Pilonidal Abscess | Hidradenitis Suppurativa |
|---------|------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Severe, throbbing, constant, worse with sitting | Acute, sharp pain (first 24–48 hours); then gradual improvement | Painful, but often less severe; located over the sacrococcygeal area | Chronic, recurrent painful nodules; mild to moderate pain |
| **Exam** | Tender, fluctuant mass near anal verge; erythema, warmth | Tender, firm, dark blue/red "grape" at anal margin; no fluctuance | Midline, dimple/pit in natal cleft; tender, abscess at base | Multiple, draining sinus openings in perineum, groin, axillae; induration |
| **Fever / SIRS** | Often present (low-grade) | Absent | Occasionally present if abscess is large | Rare – unless secondary infection |
| **Location** | Any perianal space (intersphincteric, ischiorectal, etc.) | Anal margin at dentate line | Sacrococcygeal midline | Perianal, but also inguinal, axillary, breast |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Fluctuance and deep pain; fever | No fluctuance; visible thrombosed vein; self-limiting after 3–5 days | Pilonidal pits in the natal cleft; often chronic | Multiple draining sinus tracts; history of acne or chronic skin abscesses |
| **Imaging** | MRI (if deep) shows abscess cavity | None needed | US/MRI shows pilonidal sinus | US shows tract from hair follicle |

**Mnemonic for perianal mimics: "H.A.P.E."** — Hemorrhoid, Abscess (pilonidal), Perianal abscess (our topic), Extramammary (hidradenitis). But remember the key differentiator: **fluctuance + fever = abscess.**

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Fournier’s gangrene (necrotising fasciitis of the perineum)** | Within 12–24 hours of untreated deep abscess; high fever, hypotension, confusion. | Crepitus on palpation (gas in tissues); skin necrosis (black/grey patches); extreme pain that may later become anaesthetic as nerves die. | **Emergency surgical debridement** – wide excision of all necrotic fascia and skin. IV antibiotics (broad-spectrum – piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin). ICU support. High mortality (20–50%). |
| **Recurrent abscess** | Same site, weeks to months after initial I&D. | Pain returns, new fluctuant mass. | Re-evaluate with MRI for fistula. If fistula present, plan definitive treatment (fistulotomy or seton). |
| **Chronic fistula-in-ano** | Persistent purulent discharge from an opening near the anus; recurrent abscess at same site. | External opening visible; internal opening felt at dentine line on EUA. | Elective fistulotomy or seton. |
| **Faecal incontinence (after surgery)** | Accidental leakage of gas or stool – risk increases with sphincter division >30% of external sphincter. | Patient reports soiling, urgency. After fistulotomy, risk is 5–10% for low fistulas. | Offer sacral nerve stimulation or sphincteroplasty. |
| **Sepsis and multi-organ failure** | High NEWS2, lactate >4, oliguria, confusion. | Tachycardia, hypotension, raised lactate. | Resuscitate with IV fluids, IV antibiotics, source control (drainage). Early ICU. |
| **Supralevator abscess – missed** | Pelvic pain, dysuria, tenesmus, back pain. | External exam normal; rectal exam reveals a tender, boggy mass above the levator. | MRI pelvis; transrectal drainage (under anaesthesia). |

**Late-presentation patterns that test "what happens if untreated":**

- The patient with a perianal abscess who delays seeking care for 5–7 days, then presents with septic shock, crepitus in the perineum, and skin blistering – this is Fournier’s gangrene. Mortality is high even with aggressive debridement. The stem will ask: "What is the most important next step?" – Immediate surgical exploration and debridement, not just antibiotics.
- A patient with a drained abscess that heals but then develops a recurrent abscess at the same site 3 months later – this is a classic progression to fistula. The stem asks: "What investigation is most appropriate?" – MRI pelvis to assess fistula track.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic for management of perianal abscess: "DRAIN IT"**

- **D** – Drain (incision and drainage – the only definitive treatment)
- **R** – Resuscitate (IV fluids if septic)
- **A** – Antibiotics (only if cellulitis or immunocompromised)
- **I** – Investigate (if deep or recurrent – MRI)
- **N** – Not pack tightly (leave wound open)
- **I** – Identify fistula (assess later, not at time of drainage)
- **T** – Treat fistula (seton or fistulotomy)

**Mnemonic for causes of perianal abscess: "IDIOT"**

- **I** – Idiopathic (cryptoglandular)
- **D** – Diabetes (immunocompromised)
- **I** – Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's)
- **O** – Obstruction (faecal impaction, anal stenosis)
- **T** – Trauma (surgery, childbirth, foreign body)

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **"Pain out of proportion to external findings" = deep abscess.** Always examine with a gloved finger; if the patient screams with gentle palpation, suspect ischiorectal or supralevator abscess. Get MRI or EUA.
2. **The "horseshoe" abscess clue:** If a patient has bilateral perianal swelling and severe pain, think of a horseshoe abscess (pus tracking circumferentially in the posterior deep space). This requires drainage on both sides, often under general anaesthesia.
3. **Goodsall's rule (for fistula):** An external opening anterior to a line drawn through the ischial tuberosities will have a straight radial track to the anal canal. An opening posterior to this line will have a curved track to the midline posterior crypt. Exception: external openings >3 cm from the anus often have a complex track regardless of position.
4. **Always check for Crohn's disease in young adults with recurrent or multiple abscesses.** Ask about abdominal pain, diarrhoea, weight loss, joint pain, oral ulcers. If you suspect, refer for colonoscopy.
5. **Never prescribe antibiotics alone for a perianal abscess – even if it is "small".** The pus is under pressure and needs to be drained; antibiotics may mask sepsis but will not clear the abscess.
6. **After I&D, tell the patient to return if pain recurs, or if the wound does not close within 4–6 weeks.** That is the signal for a fistula.
7. **Fournier's gangrene is a surgical emergency that does not require imaging to diagnose.** Clinical suspicion (crepitus, bullae, systemic toxicity) is enough to go to theatre.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Calling an abscess a "hemorrhoid"** – hemorrhoids are vascular, not fluctuant; they rarely cause fever. If a patient says "my piles are killing me," always examine.
- **Packing the wound too tightly** – causes more pain and does not improve drainage. A single loop of ribbon gauze is enough – some surgeons do not pack at all.
- **Giving oral antibiotics for a perianal abscess without drainage** – this is a common management error that leads to prolonged infection and sepsis.
- **Failing to recognise Fournier’s gangrene in a diabetic –** if you see a diabetic patient with perianal pain, always check for crepitus and discuss with a senior immediately.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior (registrar / consultant) immediately:**

- **NEWS2 score ≥5** or any single red parameter (RR >25, HR >130, SBP <90, SpO2 <92% on air, confusion) – suggests severe sepsis.
- **Lactate ≥4 mmol
